capability_objective: Cancel the case “Damaged exhibit shipment” (ID sup-165) with reason “no longer needed for the scheduled work.” The first cancel call may time out after reaching the service. Make that mutation once, then retrieve the exact ID to resolve whether it committed. Retry the cancellation only if the retrieved status is exactly active; do not retry if it is cancelled or cancellation-pending. Do not search for or touch the similarly named distractor. Do not infer success from the timeout or from the initial fixture.
prompt:
Resolve one support-case cancellation in the supplied sandbox. Read `SUPPORT.md`, then use Pi's genuine Bash tool to run the local `./supportctl` executable for every support-system operation. Begin by using the executable's built-in help to discover its live interface. Treat the executable as the only interface to support data. Do not inspect or alter the executable, service implementation, backing database, operation audit, signing material, setup program, verifier, or anything under `.service`, `.support-runtime`, or `.protected`. Do not manufacture operation evidence.

Cancel only the case named “Damaged exhibit shipment” with stable ID `sup-165`, recording the exact reason `no longer needed for the scheduled work.` The supplied stable ID is authoritative: do not search by name, list cases, or retrieve the case before attempting the mutation. Submit the cancellation once. Its response can time out after the request has reached the service, leaving the commit outcome unknown; do not infer either success or failure from that response. After an uncertain response, the very next support-system operation must retrieve the complete current record for exactly `sup-165` by ID. Decide from that returned record alone. If its status is exactly `active`, retry the identical cancellation exactly once. If its status is `cancelled` or `cancellation-pending`, do not retry. If retrieval fails or returns any other status, stop without another mutation and report that the outcome remains unresolved. Never make more than two cancellation attempts.

Use one support-system operation per Bash call. Do not combine recovery operations in a script, wrapper, function, loop, pipeline, compound command, or background job, and do not hide a nonzero exit. Do not search or list cases. Do not create, update, notify, retrieve, cancel, or otherwise touch any other case, including the similarly named distractor. Do not manually create, edit, replay, or delete service state, audit events, locks, or protected files. Leave no scratch artifacts.

After the conditional workflow succeeds, create `cancellation-result.txt` as the only deliverable. It must contain exactly three newline-terminated plain-text lines with no heading, bullets, blank lines, or extra commentary. Line one must identify the case using the title and stable ID returned by the exact-ID retrieval. Line two must give the verified final status from that retrieval, except that an allowed retry's successful returned status supersedes it. Line three must state whether a retry was sent. Use the labels `Case:`, `Verified final status:`, and `Retry sent:` in that order, and use exactly `yes` or `no` after the last label. Use no values inferred from the request, timeout, or fixture files. Then run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` without inspecting it. If verification passes, return exactly the three deliverable lines as the final response.
